coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots as a novel sensing material for low-cost resistive and fast-response humidity sensors
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Ruiz, Virginia
Fernández, Iván
Carrasco, Pedro
Cabañero, Germán
Grande, Hans J.
Herrán, Jaime
coredata.dc:description: A room temperature graphene quantum dots (GQDs) based sensing material for relative humidity monitoring is presented. GQDs are synthesized by pyrolysis of citric acid and are deposited on a metallic interdigitated microelectrodes by drop-casting technology. GQDs are nanosheets around 20nm in average lateral size and an average height of 2.7±0.9nm. The response time is around few seconds and the sensitivity shows an exponential relation between 15 and 80% of RH, in resistive configuration device working at room temperature. The electrical changes as a function of the RH are related to the capillary condensation produced on the surface of the GQDs. Due to this physical behavior, a resistive path is formed between the microelectrodes and the condensed water vapor, and the resistivity of the system diminishes as the RH increases. A comparison on real time between the sensor developed and a commercial device corroborates the potential application of the novel sensor presented in this work.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Humidity sensors
Capillary condensation
Resistive device
Room temperature

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrical characterization of GQD-based sensors under different RH atmospheres

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Excitation spectra at 463nm and emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths of the GQDs. Inset: photograms of GQD solutions under exposure to UV light.
FE-SEM and AFM images of GQDs and corresponding height profile and height distribution. Inset of AFM image: typically encountered aggregates of GQDs.
Humidity dynamic response of the GQD-based sensor (top) and a commercial sensor (down).
GQD-based sensor response as a function of RH.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots as a novel sensing material for low-cost resistive and fast-response humidity sensors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: IK4-CIDETEC, Materials Division, P° Miramón 196, E-20009 San Sebastián, Spain

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A room temperature graphene quantum dots (GQDs) based sensing material for relative humidity monitoring is presented. GQDs are synthesized by pyrolysis of citric acid and are deposited on a metallic interdigitated microelectrodes by drop-casting technology. GQDs are nanosheets around 20nm in average lateral size and an average height of 2.7±0.9nm. The response time is around few seconds and the sensitivity shows an exponential relation between 15 and 80% of RH, in resistive configuration device working at room temperature. The electrical changes as a function of the RH are related to the capillary condensation produced on the surface of the GQDs. Due to this physical behavior, a resistive path is formed between the microelectrodes and the condensed water vapor, and the resistivity of the system diminishes as the RH increases. A comparison on real time between the sensor developed and a commercial device corroborates the potential application of the novel sensor presented in this work.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which are graphene sheets smaller than 100nm, have been recently attracting tremendous attention due to their unique electronic and optical properties arising from quantum confinement and edge effects [1–5]. In addition to these properties, their chemical inertness, biocompatibility, low toxicity and strong photoluminescence have triggered the use of GQDs in a number of applications such as bioimaging [6], OLEDs [7], fuel cells [8], photovoltaic devices [9], composites [10] and biosensors [11].

In the specific field of sensing, different types of GQD-based sensors have been proposed that benefit from their particular optical or electronic properties. Zhao et al. [12] developed an immunosensor for the detection of human immunoglobulin based on quenching of the GQD photoluminescence by luminescence resonance energy transfer (LRET) as sensing mechanism. Quenching of GQD fluorescence as sensing mechanism has also been applied to the detection of several analytes of environmental relevance such as phosphate [13] or free chlorine [14] in water. Li et al. [15] developed a Cd2+ sensor based on electrochemiluminescence observed in GQDs.

On the other hand, other authors have developed electrochemical biosensors for the detection of single-stranded DNA [16] or glucose [17] exploiting the excellent conductivity, large surface-to-volume ratio and electrochemical response of GQDs. A different type of sensing devices relying on the singular electronic properties of GQDs are single electron transistor (SET) based charge sensors, which are switching devices that use controlled electron tunneling to amplify a current [18]. Besides charge detection, electronic sensors for the detection of humidity and pressure have been demonstrated based in electron-tunneling modulation by humidity and pressure in a percolating network of GQD embedded in a polyelectrolyte microfiber [19]. Compared with traditional graphene-based sensors, the GQD-polyelectrolyte based electronic sensor exhibits one order of magnitude higher current modulation (43-fold) and operates under a lower RH (Relative Humidity) range: 0–40% RH.

In addition to GQDs, other nanomaterials have been applied as sensing elements for humidity control applications, due to its low cost and functionality. Other superior properties of nanostructures as sensing materials include a high surface/volume ratio, fast kinetics and sensor response, high sensitivity, reduced device size and room temperature operation, which allows diminishing the power consumption of the system [20,21]. In this context, sensors with several functional materials (ZnO [21–26], MgCr2O4 TiO2 [27], TiO2 [28], SnO2 [29], In2O3 [30], poly(vinylalcohol)KOHH2O [31], Cd-doped ZnO [20], ZnOTiO2 [32], p-ZnO/n-Si [33], graphene [34], graphene oxide [35] and reduced graphene oxide [36]) have been produced by different methods (chemical vapor deposition, pulse laser deposition and screen-printing technology, among others) [21,24–26,31–33], showing different performance for humidity sensing.

In this work, the analytical properties of room temperature GQD-based resistive sensors under different humidity concentrations are presented and discussed. We report the good performance for humidity sensing of plain GQD networks prepared by a simple and low-cost method consisting on drop casting few microliters of GQD aqueous solutions onto interdigitated electrodes. As the interaction between water molecules and GQD surface takes place at room temperature, the humidity sensing mechanism is based on physiadsorption phenomena.

Citric acid was purchased from Panreac. All reagents were analytical grade and used without further purification. Solutions were prepared with ultrapure water of Synthesis A10 from Millipore (18MΩcm) (Massachusetts, USA).

GQDs were synthesized by pyrolysis of citric acid according to a protocol recently reported elsewhere [37]. Briefly, 2g of citric acid was placed in a beaker and heated to 220°C until it turned from solid to colorless liquid in a first stage and gradually to pale orange. The orange liquid was added dropwise into 50mL of 0.25M NaOH solution under vigorous stirring. A neutral (pH 7) aqueous solution of GQDs was obtained, which was stored at 4°C.

The obtained aqueous solution of GQDs, without any further purification treatment, was deposited by drop-casting (50μL drop, 5 times) on a patterned metallic interdigitated microelectrode. The final conductometric device was subjected to 5min air annealing at 100°C to eliminate the solvent of the GQDs solution.

AFM images of GQDs on mica substrates were obtained by tapping mode at room temperature using a Molecular Imaging PicoScan scanning probe microscope equipped with a nanosensors tips/cantilever, at a resonance frequency of ∼330kHz, with a spring constant of about 42N/m and a tip nominal radius lower than 10nm. Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Cary Eclipse Varian fluorescence spectrophotometer. Photographs of GQD solutions under UV light were taken in a UVP imaging system (BIO DOC-IT 220). FE-SEM images of GQDs on FTO substrates were analyzed using a Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (ULTRA plus ZEISS FESEM).

Electrical characterization was performed inside a sealed plastic chamber by a Keithley 2400 sourcemeter. Tests were carried out under different RH at room temperature (25±1°C). The target humidity atmosphere was obtained by means of a mixing system consisting of mass flow controllers (MFCs) from Bronkhorst Hi-Tech controlled by a PC. A Dynamic Data Exchange communication was established between the computer and the MFCs operated by Labview©. Inside the chamber, a commercial humidity sensor (LinPicco™ A05 Basic, Capacitive Humidity Module) was used as a humidity indicator in order to control the different atmospheres.

Solutions of GQDs emit blue light when excited with UV light, as shown in the photograph in Fig. 1 . Interestingly, GQDs exhibit photoluminescence (PL) with a maximum emission wavelength that is nearly excitation-independent, as can be seen in the emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths shown in Fig. 1. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths are 376 and 463nm, respectively.

AFM (Fig. 2 ) indicates that GQDs are nanosheets of approximately 13±3nm in lateral size estimated from the flat part of the height profile and an average height of 2.7±0.9nm. The height distribution is shown in the histogram in Fig. 2. Additionally, FE-SEM (Fig. 2) analysis indicates that GQDs are nanosheets of 22±4nm in lateral size. It is pointed out that the estimated value of the lateral size by AFM depends considerably on how the height profile is measured so it could be concluded that the small variation between the two techniques corroborate the average size estimated around 20nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrical characterization of GQD-based sensors under different RH atmospheres
ce:section.ce:para: In order to get a stable sensor signal, the operation voltage has been fixed at 1V in a pulse mode, 1s of period and 50% of duty cycle. Under continuous operation voltage the sensor baseline was not stable. The instability is related to the temperature gradient produced within the interface between the water vapor molecules and the GQD. The Joule effect associated to the electrical current density is responsible for this phenomenon, which can be minimized using pulse operation mode. A thermodynamical equilibrium is reached in this typical electrical technique very often implemented in sensors signal conditioning.

Fig. 3 shows the dynamic sensor response at different RH conditions for the GQD-based sensor and a commercial device, respectively. The time necessary to fill/evacuate the chamber was estimated to be around 1min due to the step data acquisition (30s) and the response time of the commercial device (lower than 5s, datasheet parameters, DigiPicco™ Capacitive Humidity Module). In this context, the GQD sensors show excellent response times (less than 5s) according to the commercial sensor datasheet parameters. The fast system kinetics is in good agreement with the reduction of diffusion effects between the target gas and the bulk of the material occurring when going from classic coating systems to nanostructured materials [21–24]. At RH higher than 80%, saturation behavior has been observed in the sensor response. It is believed that this fact is in relation to the saturation produced on the surface of the GQDs, where the maximum amount of water molecules would be adsorbed.

As a figure of merit, the baseline of the GQD-based sensor and the commercial one show similar behavior, indicating that GQD-based sensor has good stability and no drift effects are observed. It is worth mentioning that electronics and a temperature correction sensor are integrated in the commercial system. The excellent kinetics behavior of GQD-based sensor, and consequently the possibility of sensing at room temperature, allows sensor integration in mobile and portable systems due to the low power consumption of the device (around a maximum of 4μW pulsed operation voltage).

As it can be observed in Fig. 3 (humidity dynamic response GQD-based sensor), the measured electrical current of the system increases (resistance decreases) as humidity does. This physico-chemical behavior is related to the capillary condensation produced at the surface of nanomaterials (particle size smaller than 100nm) [21–26,38]. At high humidity, a conductive water path dominates the equivalent resistance of the circuit and an increase of the current density is observed. This effect is not observed in other nanosystems that are not based on nanoparticles size lower than 100nm [23,33] where an inverse behavior is observed.

In this context, Fig. 4 shows the GQD-based sensor response at different RH values, where RH values are obtained from the commercial sensor (x-axis) and electrical current values are measured from the GQD-based sensor (y-axis). The robustness of the GQD-based system is illustrated by the error bars obtained from the dynamic curves. Relative standard deviation (RSD) was 0.86–1.33% for RH and 2.63–8.91% for GQDs (n =12) depending on the RH range. It is worth pointing out the accuracy and non-overlapping of the experimental points.

From a theoretical point of view, the sensor response in Fig. 4 can be fitted to an exponential equation (I 0 and a are constants related to thermal equilibrium conditions [22,23,33]):
(1)
I =
I 0
exp ( a ⋅ R H )
given by the following expression (R 2 =0.9689):
(2)
I = 0.0288 exp ( 0.0647 R H )

This exponential dependence on RH is related to the capillary condensation, which takes place in nanoparticles between 2 and 100nm [38]. Additionally, it is well known that the depletion region between the GQDs and the target gas is also modeled by an exponential curve due to the potential barrier formed the GDQs, and the work–function relationship [22,23,38,39]. In this context, the sensor response to humidity is due to the conductive path formed among the GQDs and the water molecules (capillary condensation related to physiadsorbed phenomena). The electrical current formed between the electrodes is transported via the water molecules adsorbed on the surface (conductive path and water dipole moment). These physical behaviors (Eqs. (1) and (2)) support the suitability of the theoretical approaches proposed to explain the experimental results (Fig. 4).

Regarding the GQD-based sensor response, it can be graphically observed how the conductivity of the system increases with the conductive water path formed on the electrodes. As RH increases, the water path will be formed by more and more water molecules and the conductivity of the system will be higher. In absence of water, the resistive path is out of range due to the high overall resistance introduced in the system by the potential barrier between GQD. Experimental results presented in Fig. 4 corroborate the sensing mechanism proposed, as the experimental data can be fitted to the theoretical exponential behavior associated to the capillary condensation phenomena (Eq. (1)).

It is worth putting in context the sensing performance of the GQD-based sensor presented here in relation with other graphene-based sensing materials for RH monitoring previously reported. Thus, Jung et al. [36] first evaluated graphene oxide as sensing material for RH (without sensor development) while later Yang et al. [34] and Borini et al. [35] developed RH impedimetric sensors using graphene–polyelectrolyte composites and graphene oxide, respectively. As operation conditions, the former was devoted only to low RH ranges (0–30%) and the latter covers a larger RH range (30–70%) similar to the sensor presented in our work. As for the sensor kinetics, our GQD-based sensor exhibits comparable response rate (around a few seconds) to the graphene oxide based impedimetric sensor [35]. That is, with a similar sensing performance, the GQD-based sensor configuration presented here, which is based on a resistive working principle, represents a more attractive alternative in terms of cost, integration and power consumption than the impedimetric one.

A novel resistive GQD-based sensor for RH monitoring at room temperature has been developed. The response time is estimated around 5s and the sensitivity shows an exponential dependence with the RH in the measured humidity range (15–80% RH). The measured electrical changes are related to the depletion region formed between the GQDs and the water molecules at different RH concentrations. The sensing mechanism is based on capillary condensation of the water molecules on the GQDs surface. Due to this fact, a conductive water path dominates the equivalent resistance of the circuit, and the current density increases as the RH does. The experimental results fit the theoretical exponential curve, according to the model proposed. Validation of the developed sensor response against a commercial device corroborates the potential of the GQD-based sensor. Moreover, the possibility of sensing at room temperature allows the integration in mobile and portable systems due to the low power consumption of the device (around a few μW).

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the Spanish MICINN (V.R. Ramón y Cajal Program), the Basque Government (Etortek Program, nanoIKER project IE11-304) and the project Hyper (Hybrid Neuroprosthetic and Neurorobotic Devices for Functional Compensation and Rehabilitation of Motor Disorders) under program Consolider-Ingenio 2010–2014, is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr. Virginia Ruiz received her PhD in Materials Chemistry at the University of Burgos (Spain) in 2002. Since then, she has held post-doctoral fellowships and senior researcher positions at the University of Warwick (UK), Aalto University (Finland) and IK4-CIDETEC (Spain) working on synthesis, functionalization and applications of metallic and carbon nanomaterials and nanocomposites in several EU and national projects. She is co-author of over 65 scientific papers in SCI journals, 1 review and 2 book chapters related to nanomaterials science, electrochemistry and energy conversion.

Iván Fernández got his Electrónic Engineer Bachelor Degree by the University of País Vasco (UPV/EHU) in 2005 and the Automatic and Electronic Engineer Master Degree by the University of Mondragón (MU) in 2008. He has extensive experience in printing techniques of materials for different plastic electronics applications with Inkjet and Screen Printing. At present, his activity is focused on the design and manufacture of pressure sensors along with the development of signal conditioning circuits and connectivity systems thereof.

Dr. Pedro Mª Carrasco received his PhD at the University of Basque Country in 2009. He has over 10 years experience in the Nanomaterials Unit of the Materials Division at IK4-CIDETEC, San Sebastian, working in the synthesis and characterization of materials. Currently, his research interests are nanotechnology and nanomaterials.

German Cabañero Sevillano received his degree in Material Science Engineering at the University of Navarra. He is a New Materials Department Manager at IK4-CIDETEC specialized in Nanotechnology, Biomaterials, Sensors and Photonics. He generates project ideas and actively contributes to developments and strategy. Moreover, he contributes to the maintaining of state of the art skill, facilities and infrastructure, e.g. evaluation and implementation of new technologies and concepts. He has worked in a wide range of National and European projects (Transportation, Mining, Construction, Energy, etc.). Before working at IK4-CIDETEC, he worked as R & D Manager at Leading Enterprises Group and CTC.

Dr. Hans-Jürgen Grande is CTO at IK4-CIDETEC. He has PhD in Chemistry Science since 1998 (University of the Basque Country). During the last 12 years, he has managed a large number of R&D projects at national and international level. In addition, he has published a large number of scientific articles in the field of electrochemistry. He is a member of the Technical Committee at IK4 Research Alliance.

Dr. Jaime Herrán received his first class honors degree in Physics at the University of Cantabria in 2004 and his PhD at the University of Navarra in 2008. He was working in the communication engineering department of the University of Cantabria (2003–2004), the Microsystems unit of the CEIT and Tecnun, University of Navarra (2005–2010) and in 2008, he was working as a postdoc in the Microsystems Technology division of the CSEM in Neuchâtel (Switzerland) during 1 year. Since 2010, he is a researcher and a project manager in the Sensors Unit of the Materials Division at IK4-CIDETEC, San Sebastián, Spain. His research interests are solid-state Microsystems, nanotechnology and printed electronics (for further contact: jherran@cidetec.es).
